title: Packed fixed-field preallocation can overflow signed element count

direct_impact.type: signed integer overflow leading to parser abort
direct_impact.operation: packed fixed-width field growth overflows old_entries + new_entries and passes an invalid size into repeated-field growth
direct_impact.bytes: trigger demonstrated when extending a repeated fixed32 field from INT_MAX by one additional packed element

exploitability.reliability: high once the destination message is already near INT_MAX elements

chain_potential.standalone: denial of service in services that call MessageLite::MergeFrom* on attacker-controlled protobuf bytes while retaining extremely large repeated fixed fields
chain_potential.with_other_bugs: limited chaining value because the confirmed consequence is a checked abort, but the state corruption could become more useful if a non-checking build exposes a weaker container-growth path

justification.AV: N - The validated entry points include public MergeFromString and MergeFromCodedStream APIs that can process network-delivered protobuf bytes.
justification.AC: H - Exploitation requires the target message object to already contain a repeated fixed-size field near INT_MAX elements before the malicious merge occurs.
justification.PR: N - No privileges are required to send the triggering serialized field once the service holds the prerequisite state.
justification.UI: N - The failure occurs during routine merge processing.
justification.S: U - Impact is confined to the vulnerable service process.
justification.C: N - No confidentiality impact was demonstrated.
justification.I: N - The confirmed build aborts on corrupted size handling before any attacker-controlled data modification beyond the crashing operation was shown.
justification.A: H - The reproduced effect is a deterministic service-killing abort through the public library API.

notes: This is the only one of the three findings validated against the shipped libprotobuf.a public API surface in this workspace.
Severity remains MEDIUM because the precondition is extreme and the demonstrated impact is denial of service rather than memory disclosure or code execution.
